data.title: National Storage Affiliates: Several Catalysts Could Unlock Value In This Oversold REIT
data.description:
Introduction

National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA) is now trading around the levels from the pandemic, offering a solid opportunity thanks to their PRO-Transition moves combined with potential for macro improvements thanks especially to rate cuts and improving occupancy rates, although we should be mindful that the dividend payout ratio is above 100%.

NSA is a REIT with ownership interests in 1,069 self-storage properties across the US, with ~66% of them in the Sunbelt, which ultimately exposes it to the economy since their business is basically an “annex” to overall economic activity. This explains the weakness we’ve seen in recent years, but with rate cuts now advancing and the economy potentially improving from here, we could be looking at a rebound.

Catalysts for Value Creation

As mentioned, one of the big catalysts that can drive internal improvements could be the company's pivot toward internal management, which should drive long-term efficiency and improve margins going forward.

With the company completing the internalization of the externally-operated properties last year (what they call the PRO-Transition) and taking a direct operating approach that can improve their margins in the long run, NSA’s CEO also mentioned during the Q3 Earnings Release that the company’s capital recycling efforts (~$608 million spent since the beginning of 2023) and expected turnaround in supply-demand balance expected from 2026, noting that:

While the current operating environment remains competitive, the backdrop for self storage going forward is the best that we have seen in a number of years. Given the potential positive impacts on the housing market from expected interest rates cuts and a more favorable supply environment, we expect to see an improvement in self storage fundamentals supported by an improving supply/demand balance in 2026 and beyond. Our focus over the past several quarters has been to best position NSA to take advantage of this expected upturn through capital recycling and improved operational efficiency. We are ready to execute in this environment and excited about the opportunities ahead.

This optimism is further supported by supply-side dynamics, including the expectations of annual completions declining below the long-term average, which could be great if combined with improving demand thanks to better economic conditions.

From a macro perspective, rate cuts are a massive potential tailwind for NSA's valuation - as it is for many other REITs - since it benefits their business model at its core. This is why rate cuts will be key for NSA and many other REITs, as they are ultimately meant to do the opposite of the hikes that caused the current weakness, while also reducing their cost of debt as they refinance, make new debt cheaper and encourage higher investments.

The main catalyst that can come as a result would be lowering the treasury yields that could come as a result, given enough time and the right market conditions. This could basically make the risk-free rate much less attractive over time, “forcing” money to move into better risk-adjusted returns and potentially leading to a shift back into REITs.

On top of these, we’ve also seen an interesting move recently, where NSA announced a joint-venture where they committed to providing 75% of the equity capital of up to $105 million in exchange for 10% preferred equity, with potential for additional returns as the JV exits its investments in future years, with the company mentioning that it expects the new entity to deploy the capital over the next two years. The company’s CFO noted during the Q3 Earnings Release that they don’t expect the 10% to be achieved very early on, but he expects it to be reached by year 3 on average, with potential to get up to 14% total return, as the CEO added:

Yes. I mean certainly, there's an initial 10% and then upon exit of a particular part of this venture, there's a chance for us to earn up to about a 14% total return somewhere in that neighborhood of where we want to be potentially. But right now, it's a preferred [ 10% ] with an upside.

In the long-term, NSA’s services are supported by demographic tailwinds including the growing population, development of urban places, potential housing market recoveries and similar trends. Trump’s proposed 50-year mortgages could also play an important role in this industry’s future, which may currently be overlooked by the market, while NSA also noted that their markets have a higher level of homeowners compared to their peers - although it remains uncertain, for now.

This can help more people afford houses nowadays (even though the total cost is terrible over the long-term), potentially adding another pillar of support for the demand of NSA’s properties and self-storage services in general. This can matter quite a bit, as it can lead to sustained long-term demand in the homebuying market, boosting the need for “annex” services such as these storage solutions or renovation, logistics, and more.

Risks to the Thesis

Despite these catalysts supporting a recovery, we should be aware that this is far from a risk-free business. I’d also like to add that this is probably one of the most “moatless” industries for a REIT, as the cost, time and lack of specialization needed to build a self-storage property from scratch theoretically makes it very easy to meet the demand.

Something I want to reiterate is that this relative weakness seen in REITs over the past years is completely normal for the asset class, as it ultimately depends on external capital to fund its growth. Even if they can identify higher yields like the 10% equity mentioned above, funding this is also very expensive, with debt costs over 6.5% in some cases and their own stock coming at a ~7.74% cost (the dividend they’d have to issue for every new share issued).

This is a monumental difference compared to the less than 3% or 4% rates we can see on their older notes, while the stock used to trade at ~4% yields before the pandemic, so their cost of external capital was significantly cheaper, with things changing significantly due to rate hikes - which is, again, normal, and it’s why the asset class tends to underperform during high-rate environments, especially since their interest expenses also grow and their tenants/clients can have issues, as we’ve seen in many industries.

But again, I’ll mention again that these things are not instant. Even though we can see the strong correlation between rates and 10-year treasuries with the naked eye, the significant short-term volatility is just as visible, and don’t forget that things like the market’s opinion/expectations also play very important roles, while rate cuts are not the only factor going into this.

This can take years to be reflected in the company’s financials, occupancy rates and spreads, especially after the US Government went through its longest shutdown in history, which caused the near-term future of these cuts to be questioned once more, although we’ve seen some easing for that as some Fed officials signaled they see the possibility of a December cut.

As a REIT especially in this industry, they ultimately depend on the economy, with their high amount of debt and relatively high dividend yield making it very tough for them to issue at convenient rates, limiting their returns and increasing the risks in case of worsening economic conditions, which is likely why they’re going for asset recycling now instead of debt/stock issuing - which is a good decision.

Financials

Based on NSA’s latest report, we can see a drop in Core FFO YoY, although the 9M level is down by only 1.4% compared to 2024, in part as a result of a drop in occupancy to 85%, slightly lower rental revenue per occupied square foot and rising operating expenses leading to lower margins.

NSA maintained their guidance following the cut in Q2, with midpoint Core FFO per share and unit of $2.20, meaning a Core FFO of about $297.5, placing them at a ratio of around 12.74, which is not bad.

Financially, based on NSA’s latest report, we see a relatively normal position for a REIT, with a relatively high amount of debt but also a lot of assets that can more than cover it, especially since the value on the balance sheet of these properties is depreciated and not updated to their current value.

As we can see, NSA’s debt is well spread throughout the future, with no year coming with a significant maturity that would warrant concern or significant disruption, likely being able to refinance this year by year and benefitting from future rate cuts.

Dividends & Buybacks

NSA currently pays a ~7.74% dividend yield, which is among the highest in the industry, currently costing them about $308.3 million, meaning a payout of about 103.6%. This doesn’t necessarily mean they’ll cut it now and things can definitely improve from here, but it’s a risk to consider

NSA also repurchased ~$675 million worth of stock from 2022 to 2024, while nothing was spent on common share repurchases over the first nine months of 2025, but we should keep in mind that they still have $350.3 million left on their 2024 repurchase program approved a year ago, so there’s potential to see them continue these.

NSA also has two preferred convertibles (NSA.PR.A) and (NSA.PR.B), with a $25 liquidation preference and 6% initial coupon currently trading at a lower yield than the common shares, so I don’t see much of a reason to go for either of these over the common stock at today’s prices and respective yields.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), CubeSmart (CUBE), SmartStop Self Storage (SMA) and Global Self Storage (SELF), we see a generally higher level of leverage - which isn’t great - but also by far the best P/CF ratio and lowest ratios in almost everything else, which is an interesting position to be in.

We also see the only dividend above 100% in the group, while these peers come at payout ratios of around 70% to 80%, which could be the case for NSA in the future if things don’t improve and they decide to cut it.

Valuation

Starting with NSA’s annualized FCF of $322.21 million in 2025, let’s assume a CAGR of 7.5% over the first 5 years thanks especially to rate cuts combined with the high potential from their relatively high leverage, the potentially higher margins/lower costs from new PRO-Transition focus, asset recycling and potential improvements in occupancy and the industry as a whole, followed by a 4.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.75% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a WACC of 8% (slightly above my estimated ~7.9%, assuming a cost of equity of ~10.7%, cost of debt of ~4.8%), with $3.38 billion in net debt, $341.13 million in preferred equity and $608.92 million in noncontrolling interests, with 135.22 million diluted shares and units outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of ~$8.57 billion, an equity value of ~$4.24 billion, and an intrinsic value of $31.13 per share.

Conclusions

I rate NSA a Buy now, as they seem to be trading below fair value given their recovery potential, even though seeing a cut could result in a better entry level that can even warrant a rating upgrade. Risks remain, as the company ultimately depends on the environment, while the industry doesn’t really have a solid enough moat to warrant significant changes in supply that can’t offset the potential rising demand, but NSA’s potential upside is also quite attractive.

data.title: Himax: A Relatively Fine Balance Of Tailwinds And Headwinds Support A Neutral Stance
data.description:
Introduction

Himax Technologies, Inc. (HIMX) is a Taiwanese-based fabless semiconductor entity, which is primarily noted for its display driver IC [DDIC] solutions that are used in electronic devices (expertise which has been built up over 24 years).

This year, it’s fair to say that the HIMX stock has experienced quite a bumpy journey; while other emerging market small-caps have gained 12% on a YTD basis, and other semiconductor stocks have fared even better (up by 28% on a YTD basis), HIMX - which has a market-cap of $1.32B - has been all over the place, and has eventually lost ground by over 6%.

The quant system cohort too doesn’t appear to be big fans of HIMX, with Seeking Alpha’s own proprietary system currently assigning it with a sell rating (apparently stocks rated ‘sell’ or worse have seen an average decline of 20% p.a. over the past decade).

So, should investors take a cue and shun HIMX or are there some mitigating reasons to consider the stock?

How Is HIMX’s Business Faring?

For starters, in case you’re wondering why the HIMX stock has been volatile for much of this year, take note of its erratic YoY revenue profile, which did witness some growth earlier in the year, but is currently contracting at a pace of double-digit percentages YoY.

It doesn’t help that in HIMX’s most important market- automotive display ICs ( 50% revenue contribution), demand visibility is still low, and customers are still largely maintaining lean inventory, and following make-to-order models.

The market looks to the future, and it appears that there atleast for the next 3 quarters (2 out of the 3 quarters will see double-digit declines), HIMX will continue to witness a contraction in its revenue profile, with recovery likely to only take place in H2 of next year.

Much of HIMX’s weakness stems from subdued conditions in the broader DDIC market, where global shipments are poised to contract by 2% this year. Next year, growth will be easier to facilitate from this lower base, but then again, it will only grow at 2%, so essentially one is staring at a flattish market.

While all this is not ideal, investors can afford to take heart from HIMX’s relatively stable and resilient gross margin profile, which is poised to increase sequentially yet again in Q4.

Much of the gross margin prowess is being driven by HIMX’s ongoing thrust to broaden their expertise beyond display ICs in areas such as ultralow power AI, Smart glasses, and CPO [Co-packaged optics] which are all innately priced at higher-margin. Within these emerging areas, we think the CPO opportunity (something which NVIDIA has glorified) could prove to be a real dark horse over the next 12-18 months, as it has the potential to be an integral tool in AI data center applications. By leveraging HIMX’s proprietary wafer-level optics [WLO] technology, overheating challenges and high bandwidth demands of AI and HPC [high performance computing] can be ameliorated. Note that HIMX, alongside its partner-FOCI, are now bracing for full volume production next year with a meaningful ramp expected in 2027.

How Are HIMX’s Valuations From An Earnings and Cash flow Perspective?

Depending on which lens investors look at, HIMX’s valuation could appear pricey or cheap; let’s expand on this.

So, from an earnings perspective, HIMX now trades at a multiple of 31x, which few would argue is cheap, particularly as it translates to a 28% premium over the stock’s long-term rolling average of less than 25x. Paying a multiple of 31x would be acceptable if HIMX was on course to deliver earnings growth that could keep pace with that multiple, but that isn’t the case here.

HIMX’s forward valuation also looks pricey because its EPS base this year, is set to contract by a meaningful threshold of almost 50% from last year’s reported EPS of $0.456.

Now next year, there will likely be a strong bounceback in the EPS growth due to the weak base effect, as well as better contribution from the non-driver segments, but if you gauge HIMX’s earnings growth profile over a two-year time frame (FY24-FY26), the earnings CAGR works out to only 4%, which we don’t view as compelling enough for a business priced at +30x earnings.

However, we recognize that some investors may not be comfortable in gauging businesses from an earnings perspective, given the influence of accounting effects in skewing figures, so how about cash flow? Well, on that front, one could make the argument for HIMX coming across as quite compelling.

We say this because this year, HIMX’s FCF generation so far has proven to be quite resolute in relation to its market-cap. Basically for the first nine months of this year, the implied FCF has crossed the $100M mark and is growing at a pace of over 50% YoY.

This means at the current market-cap one can get a double-digit FCF yield, which is better than the 8.6% average that the stock has traditionally yielded over the past 5 years.

We believe FCF generation could continue to stay resilient as HIMX management has suggested that they are going to be cautious with inventory buildup (thus less pressure on working capital), while the DSO (days in sales outstanding), which reflects their collection efficiency is also trending lower at 87 days (both sequentially and annually).

How Is Himax’s Dividend Profile?

Semiconductor-focused investors may also be surprised to note that HIMX pays a handy dividend of close to 5% with is over 300bps better than the industry average (this is an even bigger blessing when you consider that less than one-fifth of semiconductor stocks pay a dividend).

However, do also note that the current yield is over 200bps lower than what HIMX stock has averaged (around 7%), and its payouts too can be very wobbly, with much of this centered around the cadence of earnings and cash commitments per year.

As you can see from the image below, the payout ratio over the last couple of years has been quite resilient at 100% and 81%, but this is not founded on strong earnings progression relative to 2022.

As highlighted previously, we know HIMX is likely to see a meaningful contraction in the FY25 EPS (-47% YoY decline), which also means the payout even if it is higher than last year won’t necessarily translate to superior absolute dividends or a higher yield.

Also note that f market participants were to pursue HIMX now, they wouldn’t get hold of the dividend, as even though HIMX reports annual results every December, its dividends (which it only pays once a year) are distributed in June/July every year.

What Do HIMX’s Technicals Suggest?

Well, the charts too point to a rather mixed positioning.

Firstly, it is rather evident that momentum players won’t be pursuing HIMX at this juncture, as the stock is trading well below its key daily moving averages of 50, 100 and 200, while those moving averages are also sloping downwards.

However, if one switches over to the larger time frame monthly chart which shows the price movements of HIMX over the last decade or so, we feel the risk-reward at this juncture does not look shabby. Since 2019, HIMX has been bouncing off an upward sloping support- the lower black line (it most recently revisited this in April), and the distance with the CMP is not too pronounced. In contrast, we have a long runway from the resistance (the upper black line).

Another reason to perhaps tilt to HIMX is that it looks attractively positioned for a mean-reversion bounce vs other options from the Nasdaq Innovators Completion Cap Index (which is passively tracked by the Invesco NASDAQ Future Gen 200 ETF). For those not familiar, HIMX is one amongst two hundred small-cap stocks that make up this index which focuses on small-caps with the most valuable patent portfolios relative to their total market-cap (for context, Himax has so far been granted over 2500 patents, with 371 patents awaiting approval across the world). At this juncture, HIMX’s current relative strength [RS] ratio is trading at a 15% discount to its long-term mean, and may witness some upward normalization.

Conclusion

As you may have inferred from the texture of this article, we feel quite conflicted about pursuing HIMX at this juncture. The company has been facing topline challenges, something that won't necessarily go away until H2 next year, its valuations look like a mixed bag, a tepid earnings outlook (which will also reflect on the absolute dividend paid) and it is not showing any signs of positive momentum on the charts. However, we like its long-term prospects in non-driver related areas (which are reflecting well on its gross margin), its ongoing improvement on the cash generation front, and the risk-reward on the long-term charts don't look too troubling. To conclude, we feel a hold rating feels apt for now.

data.title: CI&T: Investor-Friendly Capital Allocation, But Iffy Revenue Growth And AI Caution
data.description:
Investment Outlook

CI&T Inc. (CINT) reported its Q3 2025 financial results, beating revenue but missing earnings estimates.

I previously analyzed CINT in June 2025 with a downgrade to a Hold outlook due to uncertain growth conditions.

I remain a neutral Hold on CINT due to uncertainties about its revenue model uptake and AI project caution, but shareholder-friendly capital allocation and advantageous positioning in the Americas for nearshoring trends.

CI&T’s Market And Approach

CI&T provides various consulting, design and software engineering services for organizations in a wide range of industries.

Per a 2023 Grand View Research market research report, the global market for software consulting was an estimated $273 billion in 2022 and was expected to exceed $678 billion by 2030.

If achieved, this growth would represent a CAGR of around 12.1% from 2023 to 2030, a fairly robust rate of expected growth given the already large size of the industry.

The main drivers for this forecasted growth are an increasing preference for digitalization of all possible business processes, with the Asia Pacific region expected to produce the highest growth rate of all regions during the forecast period.

The industry is also focused on providing interested clients with consulting and pilot testing of next-generation generative AI technologies to help achieve these goals.

The software consulting industry has been investing in training employees to understand and provide value in this new area of potentially significant future growth.

The global software consulting industry’s historical and projected growth path is shown in the graphic below:

The firm obtains a majority of its revenue from the industries of Financial Services and Retail & Industrial goods companies located in Latin and North America, as the pie chart shows below:

Recent industry trends have shifted toward a return to mild growth for companies that have focused more on AI-driven ROI rather than general digital transformation.

However, growth has bifurcated depending on the industry vertical served, with automotive, healthcare and banking producing stronger growth for companies like CI&T, while retail and technology clients have been more restrained with their budgets.

As a result, firms with diverse geographic footprints and AI-application offerings have performed better.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Internal AI development platform, nearshoring advantages, ‘land & expand’ success, lean digital history.

Weaknesses

  • Client concentration, geographic dependence on the Americas, scale disadvantages, currency risks from Brazil operations.

Opportunities

  • Shift to value-based pricing, friendshoring trend, AI implementation wave, vendor consolidation.

Threats

  • Cautious AI project decision-making, LatAm talent cost increases, aggressive peer firm M&A.

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen despite tariff-related volatility. Operating income by quarter (red line) has moved incrementally higher on flat gross margin & SG&A costs against rising revenue.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has remained essentially flat. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have also flattened at a lower level from past cost containment efforts that appear to be ending with recent headcount additions.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have been resilient to slightly higher on stable gross margin and SG&A costs.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, CINT ended the quarter with $43.3 million in cash and equivalents and $66.6 million in total debt, all of which was long-term.

In the past 12 months, CINT’s stock price has fallen by 32% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Software & Services ETF’s (XSW) slide of 5%, with the main separation occurring starting in mid-May 2025, coinciding with its Q1 2025 financial results, as the chart indicates below.

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $66.5 million, and capital expenditures were $13.4 million.

The company paid $5.3 million in stock-based compensation ("SBC") in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was a respectable $61.2 million.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $30.4 million, so the company has been net retiring stock at a fairly solid rate.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 1.0%, a low figure.

Management’s plans for capital allocation in relation to shares show a renewal of the share repurchase program for up to five million Class A common shares.

Overall, CINT is paying low SBC and has significant free cash flow funding its ongoing share buyback program, so it is mechanically increasing EPS independent of revenue growth, definitely a shareholder-friendly approach.

Why I’m Neutral On CI&T

CI&T is producing top line revenue growth as it shifts from time & material pricing models to various other models such as value-based, fixed-price or output-based contracts.

The company has already seen improved demand from fixed-price software development contract uptake.

Furthermore, tariff policy change volatility has not stalled its growth trajectory, as management is seeing clients adapt accordingly.

The company’s current growth strategy includes hiring over 1,100 employees to support its pipeline, which management characterizes as ‘solid’, as well as its sales conversion efforts.

Regionally, while Latin America is producing strong growth, Europe and APAC are showing signs of recovery, adding to a more optimistic 2026 outlook.

The graphic below shows the frequency of various negative keywords in the most recent conference call between management and analysts:

The chart indicates that its Q4 results may be slightly slower than anticipated, and management declined to give any 2026 guidance.

As to valuation, I’ve compared CINT to Globant (GLOB), and the table below shows comparisons by major metrics:

The table shows that CINT is being valued at similar multiples to Globant despite an almost double net income margin on roughly the same forward top line revenue growth rate.

Looking ahead, the company has gone all-in on its CI&T Flow AI platform strategy and offerings, but the challenge is that many businesses are still in the pilot or "checking it out" stage and haven’t moved forward with large implementation projects.

This challenge isn’t limited to CI&T but is being faced by many consulting firms as client interest in generative AI’s potential is tempered by the reality of probabilistic GenAI systems when companies need deterministic, repeatable outcomes.

Another challenge the company faces is its transition of clients to various other pricing models, which will likely take at least one to two years, adding risks to its revenue growth dynamics.

Also, since management has been adding headcount ahead of delivery and potential demand, it represents optimism that may or may not be validated and is a risk to earnings in 2026.

So, with growth rate uncertain in 2026 due to pricing model change and employee headcount additions increasing costs, the risks for investors in CI&T are certainly not minimal.

Management’s shareholder-friendly capital allocation policies should provide a base for the stock price and the company's position in the Americas should benefit from trade rebalancing toward nearshoring.

Overall, my outlook for CI&T continues to be a neutral Hold for investors who are willing to be patient as the company navigates changing business and geopolitical environments.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending's NAV, Valuation, And Dividend Versus 11 BDC Peers - Part 1 (Includes Recommendations As Of 11/28/2025)
data.description:
Focus of Article:

The focus of PART 1 of this article is to analyze Blackstone Secured Lending’s (BXSL) recent results and compare a handful of the company’s metrics to 11 business development company (“BDC”) peers. This analysis will show past and current data with supporting documentation. Table 1 will compare BXSL’s recent net asset value (“NAV”) economic return (loss), adjusted net investment income (“NII”), stock price to annualized NII ratio, and percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized payment-in-kind (“PIK”)/deferred interest income to the 11 BDC peers. Table 1 will also provide a premium (discount) to estimated CURRENT NAV analysis using stock prices as of 11/28/2025. Table 2 will compare BXSL’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 6/30/2025 and 9/30/2025 to the 11 BDC peers. For reference, our last update on BXSL was here.

I am writing this two-part article due to the continued requests that such an analysis be specifically performed on BXSL and some of the company’s BDC peers at periodic intervals. These BDC peers include Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC), Capital Southwest Corp. (CSWC), FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), Gladstone Investment Corp. (GAIN), Golub Capital BDC Inc. (GBDC), Main Street Capital Corp. (MAIN), Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp. (OCSL), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), SLR Investment Corp. (SLRC), TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (TPVG), and Sixth Street Specialty Lending Corp. (TSLX).

Understanding the characteristics of a company’s investment portfolio and operating performance can shed some light on which companies are overvalued or undervalued strictly per a “numbers” analysis. This is not the only data that should be examined to initiate a position within a particular stock/sector. However, I believe this analysis would be a good “starting-point” to begin a discussion on the topic. My BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation and current price target for BXSL will be in the “Conclusions Drawn” section of the article. This includes providing a list of the BDC stocks I currently believe are undervalued (a buy recommendation), overvalued (a sell recommendation), or appropriately valued (a hold recommendation).

NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, and NII Analysis - Overview:

Let us start this analysis by getting accustomed to the information provided in Table 1 below. This will be beneficial when explaining how BXSL compares to the company’s 11 BDC peers regarding the metrics stated above. Due to the fact several BDC peers listed in Table 1 have a different fiscal year-end, all quarterly results are based on a calendar year-end. For instance, all metrics below are stated “Q3 2025” even though this does not correspond to every company’s fiscal year-end. Readers should be aware as such when the analysis is presented below.

Table 1a + 1b – NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, NII, and Capitalized PIK Analysis

(Source: Table created by me, obtaining historical stock prices from NASDAQ and each company’s NAV per share figures from the SEC’s EDGAR Database)

Table 1 above provides the following information on BXSL and the 11 BDC peers (see each corresponding column): 1) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q2 2025; 2) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q3 2025; 3) NAV per share change during calendar Q3 2025 (percentage); 4) economic return (loss) (change in NAV and accrued dividend) during calendar Q3 2025 (percentage); 5) economic return (loss) during the trailing 12-months (percentage); 6) my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 11/28/2025); 7) stock price as of 11/28/2025; 8) 11/28/2025 premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV (percentage); 9) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share during calendar Q3 2025; 10) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus the prior quarter; 11) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus calendar Q3 2024; 12) 11/28/2025 stock price to annualized NII ratio; and 13) percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income during calendar Q3 2025 (percentage).

Now that an overview has been provided, let us start the comparative analysis.

Analysis of BXSL:

Using Table 1 above as a reference, BXSL had a NAV of $27.33 per share at the end of calendar Q2 2025. BXSL had a NAV of $27.15 per share at the end of calendar Q3 2025. This calculates to a quarterly NAV decrease of ($0.18) per share or (0.66%). The majority of this minor NAV decrease was directly in relation to net realized and unrealized losses associated with a small portion of portfolio companies during the quarter. This included, but was not limited to, unrealized depreciation within DCA Investment Holdings, LLC (“DCA”), Titan Investment Company, Inc. (Titan), LD Lower Holdings, Inc. (LD Lower), and Medallia, Inc. (Medallia). When including BXSL’s quarterly base dividend of $0.77 per share and no special periodic dividend, the company had an economic return (change in NAV and accrued dividends) of $0.59 per share or 2.16% for calendar Q3 2025. It should also be noted BXSL had a trailing 12- and 24-month economic return of 10.85% and 25.51%, respectively. Both percentages were at – slightly above the covered 12 BDC peer average (a neutral - positive catalyst/trend).

BXSL’s performance during the past four quarters was mainly attributable to the following three factors: 1) minor-modest net underpayment of dividends (including special periodic dividends if/when applicable) when compared to the company’s NII/adjusted NII; 2) very minor net realized loss within a couple of exited/restructured portfolio companies; and 3) minor - modest net unrealized depreciation within the company’s active investment portfolio. This is a good transition to the next topic of discussion, an analysis of BXSL’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 6/30/2025 and 9/30/2025. To begin this analysis, Table 2 is provided below.

Tables 2a + 2b – Investment Portfolio Composition Analysis (Including Several Additional Metrics; 9/30/2025 Versus 6/30/2025)

(Source: Tables created by me, directly obtaining some figures/percentages from the SEC’s EDGAR Database [link provided below Table 1]. All remaining figures/percentages were calculated using data obtained within the SEC’s EDGAR Database)

Using Tables 2a and 2b above as a reference, BXSL had 97% and 2% of the company’s investment portfolio in senior secured first- and second-lien loans as of 9/30/2025, respectively. As such, these types of loans comprised the vast majority of BXSL’s investment portfolio. When compared to the prior quarter, BXSL’s percentage of senior secured first- and second-lien loans decreased (1%) and increased 1%, respectively. BXSL also had less than 1%, 0%, and 1% of the company’s investment portfolio in subordinated debt (unsecured loans), collateralized loan obligations (“CLO”)/credit-linked notes (“CLN”) (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants, respectively. When compared to the prior quarter, BXSL’s percentage of subordinated debt (unsecured loans), CLO/CLN (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants remained unchanged. As such, there was not a notable shift in investment portfolio composition during calendar Q3 2025.

I would also point out BXSL’s proportion of second-lien loans and subordinated debt is very low when compared to the company’s 11 BDC peers. Simply put, this is generally a positive characteristic regarding potential credit risk/recoveries in a recessionary environment (consider general credit hierarchy). I believe BXSL’s below average non-accrual percentages are direct evidence of the company’s more cautionary strategy regarding capital stack/credit hierarchy.

As of 9/30/2025, BXSL’s investment portfolio had a “fair market value (“FMV”) versus cost” ratio of 0.9912x. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this ratio was slightly below the mean of 1.0164x (a minor negative factor/trend). However, I would point out MAIN’s and GAIN’s FMV versus cost ratio of 1.1845 and 1.0903 as of 9/30/2025, respectively, “skews” the metric to the upside a bit. When compared to a ratio of 0.9944x as of 6/30/2025, BXSL’s ratio very slightly decreased during calendar Q3 2025. As noted above, this was mainly the result of unrealized depreciation in several BXSL’s portfolio companies. A more detailed “breakdown” of BXSL’s quarterly performance was recently provided to our Investing Group subscribers via a quarterly earnings assessment article.

BXSL had only 0.1% and 0.1% of the company’s investment portfolio on “non-accrual” status as of 9/30/2025; based on its amortized cost basis and FMV, respectively. When compared to the 11 BDC peers as of 9/30/2025, BXSL’s amortized cost and FMV non-accrual percentage were modestly below the mean of 2.9% and 1.4%, respectively (a positive catalyst/trend).

Since the company’s initial public offering (“IPO”) in 2021, BXSL’s investment portfolio as of 9/30/2025 has generated a cumulative realized loss of only ($0.13) per share (when based on a per share count as of 9/30/2025). BXSL’s cumulative realized loss figure was modestly more attractive when compared to the mean loss of ($0.89) per share (a positive catalyst/trend). This includes GAIN’s cumulative realized gain of $4.39 per share which skews this figure to the upside (would be a larger net loss when excluding GAIN). I believe calculating a BDC’s cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount provides an extremely useful metric when analyzing the long-term performance of management’s underwriting abilities, due diligence, expertise, and operational performance. This metric provides direct evidence BXSL’s management team has, regarding a majority of instances, continued to find attractive debt/equity investments over a long period of time which, more times than not, have ultimately delivered attractive risk-adjusted returns.

As of 9/30/2025, 0.75% of BXSL’s portfolio had debt and equity investments within the oil and gas sector (based on FMV; including certain investments in the energy sector which had “oil and gas” characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector). When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, BXSL’s oil and gas exposure was slightly below the mean of 1.45%. When compared to the prior quarter, BXSL’s exposure to the oil and gas sector fractionally decreased. Even though larger oil and gas companies benefited during most of 2022 from a net increase to commodity prices tied to inflation and the ongoing Russia/Ukraine conflict, I would point out most sector prices have largely retraced during late 2022 – 2025. In addition, smaller/private oil and gas companies are not “out of the woods” (still high-cost outlays making breakeven prices higher). This could be a short- or long-term event dependent upon many unknown geopolitical/macroeconomic variables. As such, I would remain a bit cautious considering the ramifications of this specific sector regarding high-yield/speculative-grade credit. I believe BXSL’s continued very low percentage attributed to oil and gas companies remains a positive catalyst/trend.

Once again using Table 1 as a reference, BXSL reported adjusted NII (which excludes merger-related premium amortization) of $0.882 per share during calendar Q3 2025. I prefer to track/utilize BXSL’s adjusted NII metric as opposed to NII (more indicative of net investment company taxable income [ICTI]). When comparing each company’s stock price as of 11/28/2025 to its annualized NII (or where applicable adjusted NII), BXSL had the 3rd lowest ratio at 8.39x. BXSL’s annualized adjusted NII ratio was slightly - modestly below the 12-peer ratio of 9.87x as of 11/28/2025 (a positive catalyst/trend). Historically speaking, BXSL’s price-to-earnings ratio as of 11/28/2025 was attractive/low (a positive catalyst/trend).

During calendar Q3 2025, 8.82% of BXSL’s total investment income was attributable to capitalized PIK/deferred interest income. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this was slightly above the mean of 7.33% (a slightly negative catalyst/trend). I believe it is never a positive catalyst/trend when a BDC has any portion of its accrued income classified as being capitalized/deferred. Simply put, under GAAP, capitalized PIK interest/dividend income is revenue that is currently being “booked” but has not actually been received in cash yet (deferred). In a majority of cases, capitalized PIK interest income is paid in cash at the maturity of that particular loan/when a sale occurs. However, in my experience, more times than not capitalized PIK interest income is a contractual amendment regarding a specific portfolio company who is, at the time, having operational difficulties (which increases the probably of the eventual inability of paying its loan obligations). This especially holds true when a specific debt investment had no capitalized PIK feature at the origination of a particular loan but is currently accruing 100% capitalized PIK interest income due to a loan modification/restructuring.

Simply put, in a majority of cases, it is a “slick” strategy of continuing to record accrued interest income only to write-off this capitalized interest income at a later date; usually at loan maturity by classifying that “lost deferred interest” as a reduction in the debt investment’s proceeds (a realized loss) as opposed to lowering previously accrued income by the accumulated capitalized PIK balance. I am not stating this occurs all the time but certain BDC peers tend to utilize this “phantom income” strategy regularly. In particular, Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC) has been prone to this strategy to a greater degree over the years versus all the other BDC peers within this analysis which was pointed out for years (and one of the reasons I dropped that BDC back in 2024). As such, it could be the case capitalized PIK interest income is never “completely” received in cash upon maturity/when a sale occurs. In my professional opinion, if a BDC has a large/above average portion of its investment income classified/accrued as capitalized PIK interest income, it should be seen as a potential concern regarding future performance/credit quality. In the end, one really just has to go “case-by-case” to determine the overall “viability” of a BDC actually eventually receiving this capitalized PIK/deferred interest income in the future. Something I continually monitor/track in my modeling.

A great recent example of this trend was FSK. This particular BDC peer has been carrying high – very high capitalized PIK/deferred interest income for quite some time. I previously continued to warn subscribers/readers about this negative factor/trend for a handful of quarters. I also warned of “looming” non-accruals within FSK’s investment portfolio. During calendar Q2 2025, FSK placed 4 new portfolio companies on non-accrual status. This included 3 very large portfolio companies that previously had PIK provisions. Simply put, this was not a surprise to me/my team but appeared to be quite a surprise for market participants. As a direct result, FSK’s stock price fairly recently notably dropped (I/we had FSK listed as notably overvalued/STRONG SELL prior to the company’s Q2 2025 earnings).

As of 11/28/2025, BXSL’s stock price traded at $27.59 per share. When calculated, BXSL’s stock price was trading at a premium to my estimated CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 11/28/2025; $27.10 per share) of $0.49 per share or 1.81%. This was slightly more attractive than the 12-BDC covered peer average of a premium of 3.65% (a slightly positive catalyst/trend). I continue to believe BXSL should trade at a modest – notable premium to the company’s CURRENT NAV. As such, based on my proven valuation methodology over various interest rate/economic cycles, I currently believe BXSL is the lone BDC peer that is deemed to be notably undervalued.

Comparison of BXSL’s NAV, Economic Return, Valuation, NII, and Other Metrics to 11 BDC Peers in Ranking Order:

The REIT Forum Feature

Conclusions Drawn (PART 1):

PART 1 of this article has analyzed BXSL and 11 other BDC peers in regards to the following metrics: 1) trailing 12-month economic return (loss) (good indicator of recent overall performance); 2) percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 9/30/2025 (good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk); 3) cumulative gain (loss) per share as of 9/30/2025 (great indicator of long-term performance); 4) current premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 11/28/2025) (very good indicator of overall valuation); 5) current stock price to annualized NII ratio (good indicator of overall valuation); and 6) percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income (good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk).

When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, I believe BXSL continues to outperform a majority of the company’s BDC peers I currently cover. This includes, but is not limited to, BXSL’s attractive adjusted NII per share (though, to remain unbiased, has net decreased over the prior several quarters [similar to most sector peers]), a modestly - notably below average percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 9/30/2025, a modestly more attractive cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount as of 9/30/2025, a very low exposure to the oil and gas sector (including certain investments in the energy sector which had oil and gas characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector), an above average trailing 24-month economic return percentage, and a slightly more attractive stock price to annualized adjusted NII ratio (all positive catalysts/trends). In addition, BXSL is tied as having the lowest management fees out of the externally-managed covered BDC peers at 1.00% of total assets less cash and cash equivalents. Simply put, this will continue to positively impact shareholders via lower proportionate fees versus most other externally-managed BDC covered peers.

That said, to remain non-bias, this article also highlighted BXSL had a slightly below average FMV versus cost ratio and a slightly above average capitalized PIK/deferred interest income percentage (slightly negative factors/trends).

Looking back, I previously correctly identified the very high probability of an increasing BXSL base dividend and/or special periodic dividends during 2022 - 2023. This was mainly due to the U.S. London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR)/Standard Overnight Financing Rate (“SOFR”)/PRIME very quickly moving past all floors on the asset side of the balance sheet, along with financing outstanding borrowings with longer-term, lower cost debt on the liability side of the balance sheet, during 2021.

That said, to remain non-bias, during 2024 - 2026 one had/has to continue to monitor the weighted average annualized yield on new loan originations versus exiting debt investments as there remains a pretty good “lag” in this specific metric. Something I continually track. In addition, I believe the majority of sector earnings peaked during late 2023 – early 2024 (fairly recently plateaued). The rapid NII growth that sector peers experienced during 2022 - 2023 simply did not occur during 2024 (nor will occur during 2025 - 2026). As the FOMC began to reduce the Federal Funds Rate (started in September 2024), sector earnings will GRADUALLY decrease over time. The severity of decreases will vary peer-to-peer (which I continuously project/model). This notion is already embedded in all price targets. Furthermore, credit risk will almost certainly rise (albeit mildly) throughout the sector during late 2025 – 2026 but should begin to slowly ease in late 2026 - 2027.

However, I continue to believe BXSL is better positioned to weather a potential minor - modest recession when compared to most sector peers (as highlighted in the analysis above). Dividend sustainability will be discussed/analyzed in PART 2 of this sector comparative analysis.

My BUY, SELL, or HOLD Recommendation:

From the analysis provided above, including additional factors not discussed within this article (additional metrics covered in PART 2), I currently rate BXSL as a SELL when I believe the company’s stock price is trading at or greater than a 22.5% premium to my projected CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 11/28/2025; $27.10 per share), a HOLD when trading at less than a 22.5% premium but greater than a 12.5% premium to my projected CURRENT NAV, and a BUY when trading at or less than a 12.5% premium to my projected CURRENT NAV.

Therefore, with a closing price as of 12/2/2025 of $27.44 per share, I currently rate BXSL as NOTABLY UNDERVALUED from a stock price perspective.

As such, I currently believe BXSL is a STRONG BUY recommendation.

My current price target for BXSL is approximately $33.20 per share. This is currently the price where my recommendation would change to a SELL. The current price where my STRONG BUY recommendation would change to a HOLD is approximately $30.50 per share. Put another way, the following are my CURRENT BUY, SELL, or HOLD per share recommendation ranges for BXSL (our Investing Group subscribers get this type of data on all 12 BDC (and 18 mortgage real estate investment trust [mREIT) stocks I currently cover on a weekly basis):

$33.20 per share or above = SELL (Overvalued)

$30.51 - $33.19 per share = HOLD (Appropriately Valued)

$27.81 - $30.50 per share = BUY (Undervalued)

$27.80 per share or below = STRONG BUY (Notably Undervalued)

BDC Sector Recommendations as of 11/28/2025:

Table 10 – Past and Current BDC Recommendations

(Source: Table created by me, including all past and present recommendations based on data obtained from the SEC’s EDGAR Database [link provided below Table 1a])

Table 10 above provides the following information on BXSL and the 11 BDC peers (see each corresponding column): 1) 7/18/2025 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation (pre late summer 2025 sell-off); 2) 10/10/2025 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation (pre fall 2025 rally); and 3) 11/28/2025 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation range, relative to my estimated CURRENT NAV.

I currently have 1 BDC rated as NOTABLY UNDERVALUED (STRONG BUY), 3 rated as UNDERVALUED (BUY), 6 rated as APPROPRIATELY VALUED (HOLD), 1 rated as OVERVALUED (SELL), and 1 as NOTABLY OVERVALUED (STRONG SELL). Simply put, certainly not as bullish as March - April 2020, during the summer of 2024, or more recently during April and October 2025. However, certainly not as bearish as late 2019 – early 2020 or the June - July 2025. I currently believe the sector, as a whole, is slightly undervalued - appropriately valued. I would just be mindful knowing high-yield/speculative-grade credit spreads directly impact broader asset valuations. I continue to project a net widening of spreads during late 2025 - 2026. This is something I have continued to discuss with subscribers. Important to understand.

That said, I continue to see very good - great value with BXSL (yes, even considering a projected dividend reduction at some point during 2026; discussed in PART 2).

The analysis performed above does not provide “every” catalyst/factor to consider when choosing a BDC investment. However, I believe this analysis is a good starting point to begin a discussion on the topic. Additional metrics will be analyzed in PART 2 of this article. PART 2 will take a look at BXSL’s past and current dividend rates, yields, and other similar metrics and compare the results to the 11 other BDC peers. Several of these metrics have a direct impact on future operations/results as events unfold. This includes dividend projections for all 12 peers for calendar Q1 2026/each applicable company’s next set of dividend projections.

My Personal BXSL Past + Current Stock Disclosures:

The following are my BXSL past and current stock disclosures and total returns since I have been writing on Seeking Alpha (since 2013):

Table 11 – BXSL Past + Current Stock Disclosures/Returns

Source: Taken Directly from the REIT Forum’s © Spreadsheets/Data

Final Note: All trades/investments I have performed over the past 9+ years have been disclosed to readers in “real time” (that day at the latest) via Seeking Alpha and, more recently, the “live chat” feature of our Investing Group (which cannot be changed/altered). Beginning in January 2020, I transitioned all my real-time purchase and sale disclosures solely to subscribers of the REIT Forum. All applicable public articles will still have my “main ticker” purchase and sale disclosures (just not real-time alerts). At the end of November 2025, I had an unrealized/realized gain “success rate” of 89.5% and a total return (includes dividends received) success rate of 95.3% out of 86 total past and present mREIT and BDC positions (updated monthly; multiple purchases/sales in one stock count as one overall position until fully closed out). I encourage other Seeking Alpha contributors to provide real time buy and sell updates for their readers/subscribers which would ultimately lead to greater transparency/credibility.

Simply put, a contributor’s/team’s recommendation track record should “count for something” and should always be considered when it comes to credibility/successful investing.

Understanding My/Our Valuation Methodology Regarding mREIT Common and BDC Stocks:

The basic "premise" around my/our recommendations in the mREIT common and BDC sectors is value. Regarding operational performance over the long-term, there are above average, average, and below average mREIT and BDC stocks. That said, better-performing mREIT and BDC peers can be expensive to own, as well as being cheap. Just because a well-performing stock outperforms the company’s sector peers over the long-term, this does not mean this stock should be owned at any price. As with any stock, there is a price range where the valuation is cheap, a price where the valuation is expensive, and a price where the valuation is appropriate. The same holds true with all mREIT common and BDC peers. As such, regarding my/our investing methodology, each mREIT common and BDC peer has their own unique BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation range (relative to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV). The better-performing mREITs and BDCs typically have a recommendation range at a premium to BV/NAV (varying percentages based on overall outperformance) and vice versa with the average/underperforming mREITs and BDCs (typically at a discount to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV).

Each company’s recommendation range is "pegged" to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV because this way subscribers/readers can track when each mREIT and BDC peer moves within the assigned recommendation ranges (daily if desired). That said, the underlying reasoning why I place each mREIT and BDC recommendation range at a different premium or (discount) to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV is based on roughly 15-20 catalysts which include both macroeconomic catalysts/factors and company-specific catalysts/factors (both positive and negative). This investing strategy is not for all market participants. For instance, not likely a “good fit” for extremely passive investors. For example, investors holding a position in a particular stock, no matter the price, for say a period of 5+ years. However, as shown throughout my articles written here at Seeking Alpha since 2013, in the vast majority of instances I have been able to enhance my personal total returns and/or minimize my personal total losses from specifically implementing this particular investing valuation methodology. I hope this provides some added clarity/understanding for new subscribers/readers regarding my valuation methodology utilized in the mREIT common and BDC sectors.

Each investor's BUY, SELL, or HOLD decision is based on one's risk tolerance, time horizon, and dividend income goals. My personal recommendation will not fit each reader’s current investing strategy. The factual information provided within this article is intended to help assist readers when it comes to investing strategies/decisions. Please disregard any minor “cosmetic” typos if/when applicable.

data.title: Baldwin Group's CAC Purchase: Strategically Sound, But Debt Is Concerning
data.description:
Shares of The Baldwin Insurance Group, Inc. (BWIN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing nearly half of their value. The company has struggled with an excessive valuation as margins have moderated, partially due to accounting changes. Even with the stock down substantially, Baldwin just announced an acquisition with a large equity component, suggesting that BWIN is still comfortable issuing shares here. With a major acquisition and shares down substantially, now is a good time to determine if Baldwin is attractive going forward.

With CAC, Baldwin Adds A High-Quality Business

On Dec. 2, Baldwin agreed to buy CAC Group for $1.03 billion. Baldwin is paying $438 million in cash and issuing 23.2 million shares, worth ~$589 million, and in 2030, there will be a deferred $70 million payment made. Depending on retention and performance, there could be an additional $250 million of payments. Baldwin expects to close this deal in Q1 2026, and I do not foresee any antitrust concerns.

CAC is the nation’s 35th largest insurance broker, while Baldwin is the 15th. Together, they are the 12th largest insurance broker, with ~$14 billion of annual premiums. This remains a fairly fragmented industry, with ongoing consolidation likely. With the company not even a top-5 player, regulatory pushback to this deal is unlikely to be material.

CAC has been a high-growth business. Since 2020, it has grown revenue by ~29% per year, and it has an extremely productive workforce. Its average colleague generates ~$481k in retained commissions and fees, more than $200k above the top quartile insurance broker. Importantly, its average advisor is much younger than the industry average, and at 46 years old, most of its advisors have meaningful careers ahead of them. That is an important consideration, as advisors nearing retirement may be highly productive today, but retaining those sales after retirement could prove difficult.

CAC has grown by adding advisors to sell insurance policies, as well as making these advisors more productive. In fact, 76% of advisors generate over $1 million in commissions once hitting the 3-year mark with CAC, up from 48% in 2021. Now, the biggest risk of a merger like this is that agents leave for other firms, taking much of their business with them. This is why there are $250 million of earn-outs, to insulate BWIN from higher attrition. Importantly, CAC is majority owned by its employees and advisors. With an ownership culture and a stake in the firm, the advisor base should be fairly sticky.

CAC has $310 million of revenue and ~$70 million of EBITDA. Baldwin expects to generate ~$60 million of synergies. Including run-rate synergies, Baldwin expects this transaction to increase EPS by ~20%. Management expects to achieve $10 million of synergies next year and fully realize synergies by 2028. 25% of synergies are tied to revenue, which I tend to view as more aspirational. Baldwin offers reinsurance brokerage solutions, and taking this Juniper Re offering and extending it to existing CAC clients does offer a clear path for revenue upside. Expense savings primarily come from removing duplicative headcount and functions (technology, office, etc.). I view ~$50 million as a more reasonable synergy base case, given my hesitancy to credit all revenue targets.

Debt Is Elevated

Over the past few years, Baldwin has been prioritizing getting leverage of 3-4x, and the company has made progress towards this with leverage just outside the high end of the range at 4.1x. With leverage approaching the target, Baldwin had announced an intention to authorize a $200 million buyback, but I expect repurchases to now be on hold. With ~$438 million in cash being paid at closing and CAC having ~$70 million of a standalone EBITDA, this will bring pro forma leverage towards 4.5x.

Now with $60 million of synergies, this deal would be slightly deleveraging if the synergies are fully realized, though there are also potential earnout payments. Importantly, Baldwin has no near-term maturities, with its first debt due in 2029. I expect leverage to end 2026 in the low 4x range, and I would not expect material buybacks before 2027. Overall, I view Baldwin’s financial policy as aggressive, but not dangerously so.

Overall, this deal adds scale, making Baldwin a more meaningful player in the insurance broker sector while giving it more exposure to niches like insurance products for natural resources and private equity. Additionally, Baldwin is acquiring a highly productive advisor base. That said, it leaves the company with elevated debt for longer, and buybacks are no longer likely to be a capital allocation focus. Maintaining CAC’s culture during the integration will also be essential.

Standalone Results Are Mixed

Aside from the CAC deal, Baldwin’s performance has been mixed. In the company’s third quarter, reported on Nov. 4, Baldwin earned $0.31 per share, which matched expectations, as revenue grew by 8% to $365 million. Organic revenue was up 5%, but EBITDA was flat at $73 million as the company has seen modest margin pressure. Margins were 19.8% in Q3, down 170bps from last year. The company’s backlog is sitting at a record high, and this should mostly be realized in H1 of 2026. Additionally, the company is undertaking a 3-year transformation program aimed at increasing automation. This program will cost $40 million to implement, with run-rate savings targeted at $50 million by the end of 2028.

Now, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS exclude share-based compensation, which is a noncash expense. Baldwin is not unique in doing this, but I believe it is wrong to exclude this expense. While noncash, share-based comp is a real cost in the form of dilution. Share-based comp was $22 million in Q3 and stands at $52 million year to date. If investors add back share-based compensation, adjusted EPS for 2025 would be ~$0.30 below guided levels.

Sales velocity was a strong 20%, though this was down from 22% in Q2. Accounting changes have delayed when the company will recognize revenue until some policies expire, creating a ~$7 million headwind and reducing growth by about 2%. The company is seeing some pressure on policy pricing, particularly tied to catastrophe risk, which matches what reinsurance firms have been saying. Lower insurance pricing is a net headwind for Baldwin, as Baldwin earns a commission tied to the value of the policy. If a policy's price is lower, that mechanistically reduces its commission. We have seen insurance premium inflation steadily moderate, and I expect pricing increases of 0%-3% next year, down from a peak closer to 6%, which will be a headwind for revenue growth.

Adjusted free cash flow is $76 million year-to-date. This is closer to ~$24 million if we add back share-based compensation. Baldwin ended the quarter with $90 million of cash and $1.6 billion of debt. This will pass $2 billion of debt upon completion of the CAC deal. I expect the company to gradually work to bring debt down under $1.8 billion before buying back shares. Until buybacks begin, we will likely see the share count rise 2%-3% due to share-based compensation.

In Q4, Baldwin expects to earn $0.28-$0.32 on ~$350 million of revenue. Baldwin also offered preliminary 2026 guidance of $1.66-$1.7 billion of revenue and ~$1.95-$2.10 in EPS, with free cash flow up “double-digits.” This was before the CAC deal, which ultimately should add about $0.25-0.30 of EPS. These benefits will largely be after 2026, given the time it takes to generate synergies.

With Debt Reduction The Next Focus, Shares Are A "Hold"

With slowing premium inflation and integration costs, I expect about $1.70 in 2026 EPS (adding back the cost of share-based compensation) or about $2.00 excluding share-based compensation. Shares are trading at 15x, which is at a discount to larger peers like Willis Tower Watson (WTW), which trades closer to 18x forward earnings. That said, WTW has much larger scale as well as a strong balance sheet which enables capital returns. Baldwin has a need to focus on debt reduction, and its share count will steadily grow.

As a result, I struggle to see material multiple expansion. Owners of CAC shares who are receiving BWIN stock may also choose to sell shares now that their stake is substantially more liquid, a further headwind to multiple expansion. This acquisition is sound, but with further debt, BWIN’s financial position leaves me as a "Hold," especially with under $50 million of underlying free cash flow (ex-SBC), meaning that deleveraging will be a prolonged process.

data.title: Was It The Week That Wasn't? - Weekly Blog # 917
data.description:
Originally published on November 30, 2025

Does 3½ US Trading Days make a week?

The bullish media and "street" pundits were thrilled that the 3½-day trading week restored early November losses to the popular stock averages, although they were disappointed the rise did not break through to new highs. Looking at the results, they resembled a week from a younger bull market.

Reality may have been the problem

At least one analyst calculated that if you eliminated all “AI”-related activity since 2019, “the market” is probably down. This suggests that since 2019, we have experienced a slowly declining bear market. The Conference Board’s measure of confidence recently dropped to 88.7%, which was more than the expected reading of 93% and the prior reading of 95.5%. HP (HPQ), the old equipment producer part of Hewlett Packard, joined many other large employers in announcing expectations of a 10% job cut. The American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) sample survey for the last three weeks reported bullish projections of 32.0%, 32.6% and 31.6%, respectively, for the next six months. Their bearish projections remained in the 40-49% range.

Regular subscribers to these blogs have learned of my concerns about the declining quality of balance sheets, a warning sign of economic turmoil. One measure of this is the much larger growth in volume on the NASDAQ vs. the “Big Board”. In the short Friday trading session, the decline in volume on the NASDAQ was twice as large as the percentage decline on the NYSE.

Two Causes of Economic Turmoil

As with the run-up to the 1929 crash, the Roaring Twenties led to overconfidence (AI?) and unsound leverage (Private Capital?). The organizational hollowing-out is causing an increase in execution risk. Governments, universities, businesses, and families reacting to increasing financial strain are looking to improve efficiencies. Efficiency, not effectiveness, is measured by output versus input. Many have assigned revenues or other outputs to those at both the top and bottom of the production ladder. The people in the middle, mostly supervisors/middle management, have not been credited with the output assigned to those at the top and bottom and have been reduced or eliminated entirely. One glaring example is the federal government, although this trait is found throughout society. The President has had difficulty getting many of his actions approved by the courts. In numerous cases, there was insufficient careful staff work, which would have phrased efforts better or would have raised internal discussion instead of simple loyalty in attempting to execute flawed orders. This is a pattern exhibited in other organizations.

Thoughts?

Original Post

data.title: Veritone: AI Pipeline Grows, But Sales Execution Is The Open Question (Upgrade)
data.description:
Characterizing 2025 as the year of the AI gold rush probably wouldn't be an overstatement. This is the year where every company has been challenged to demonstrate how they are using AI or selling it in order to get acceptance in the public markets. And for software companies in particular, the race is on to prove which products can remain relevant in an AI age that will be dominated by agentic products.

Amid this backdrop, Veritone, Inc. (VERI) has seen a surprising resurgence. The company describes itself as the "AI operating system," and has evolved beyond its legacy ad-tech business to build up a new book of business in enterprise software. But with the stock up ~60% since the start of the year, we have to ask ourselves: is the stock market putting the cart before the horse in this small-cap, largely untested stock?

I last wrote a sell article on Veritone in October, when the stock was still trading in the high $5s. Since then, Veritone has dropped about ~10%, while the company has also released strong Q3 results that showcased improving consumption ARR and software revenue growth that suggests some level of success for its new AI platform. I'm upgrading Veritone to a neutral rating: though I think Veritone's return to revenue growth puts a floor on recent share price performance, Veritone still also has a choppy history for execution, which doesn't make this a comfortable long-term hold for me.

"AI Tokenization" strategy is yielding consumption ARR gains

In early December, Veritone hosted a conference in which the company laid out its vision for its AI product as well as its go-to-market approach. This acted as a major upside catalyst for the stock, driving a ~20% rally from the mid-$4s to the mid-$5s.

Veritone envisions its "AIware" operating system to be the layer that helps companies transform unstructured data into "tokens" that are able to be ingested by AI models for analysis and tasks. Unstructured data, as a reminder, is data that can't fit neatly into rows and columns like an Excel spreadsheet or traditional database: it's the video, image, and audio files that continue to see rapid generation and which businesses view as containing valuable insights.

Summarizing this vision during the conference, CEO Ryan Steelberg noted as follows:

Through VDR, we have established ourselves as a premier data and model training partner for many of the largest hyperscalers and foundational model developers. We transform, again, raw, unstructured audio and video into high-quality tokenized data sets that can be used to train and fine-tune the world's most advanced AI models.

VDR is a new product line that is being sold into both public sector and commercial enterprise customers. With a qualified near-term pipeline and bookings already surpassing $40 million, VDR is emerging as a critical partner to many global hyperscalers and the next generation of AI model developers.

As we've stated on previous calls, we are confident by the end of the year, we will hold active contracts or projects with nearly every major hyperscaler in the market, further solidifying VDR's position as a critical enabler in the unstructured AI training data ecosystem.

Our tokenization platform not only powers our AI workflows and customer applications today, but now serves as the foundation for a powerful new monetization framework. The data as a currency era has arrived and Veritone is uniquely positioned to capitalize."

The company further believes that the "tokenization" of data as an asset will become a $13.5 trillion industry by 2030. Now, Veritone has made claims about being an AI company long before the AI gold rush took off: but what makes me less pessimistic on Veritone now (versus my previous Sell rating) is that we're starting to see evidence of the company's success play out in its quarterly results.

The chart below breaks out Veritone's revenue in its most recent quarter by product and by customer segment.

Total revenue grew 33% y/y to $29.1 million, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $28.4 million (+29% y/y) by a four-point margin. Veritone's software revenue, meanwhile, jumped 55% y/y to $22.8 million.

This is a massive turnaround from Q2, where Veritone's total revenue declined -2% y/y and software revenue grew just 12% y/y, hence my change in sentiment on this stock. Furthermore, we're also seeing a boost in ARR. The company's SaaS ARR was roughly flat at ~$50 million, but its consumption ARR jumped 54% sequentially to $18.8 million, which is a signal of healthy deal activity for its consumption pricing-based AI platform.

New bookings also rose 30% y/y to $21.5 million, which bodes well for further revenue acceleration. We're still concerned that the company is shedding total software customers: but as long as the company is landing higher-spending AI customers (which its ARR and bookings metrics seem to suggest), Veritone is still moving in the right direction.

CEO Steelberg noted that the VDR platform also has $40 million of qualified opportunity, representing roughly 2x the current quarter's bookings. Per his remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Our qualified VDR pipeline and bookings now exceed $40 million, reflecting 100% growth quarter-over-quarter. Importantly, that figure represents only current bookings and near-term opportunities. Our total VDR pipeline now spans multiple sectors, regions and time horizons, positioning Veritone for sustained growth as AI developers increasingly move beyond open web data in favor of proprietary multimodality data sets, precisely the domain VDR was built to serve.

Looking forward, we are confident that by the end of 2025, Veritone will hold active contracts or projects with every major hyperscaler in the market."

Another positive to note: Veritone negotiated a debt paydown, and after the transaction it's in a far more benign liquidity position, with $34 million of cash and $45 million of debt, or just $11 million in net debt.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices in the low $5s, Veritone trades at a market cap of $478.3 million; tacking on the $11 million of net debt on Veritone's balance sheet after its debt paydown gives us an enterprise value of $489.3 million.

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting Veritone to generate $131.0 million in revenue, representing 19% y/y growth. This positions Veritone's valuation at 3.7x EV/FY26 revenue.

Software companies that are growing revenue at a mid/high teens pace tend to trade at a mid/high single-digit multiple of revenue. Examples include Atlassian (TEAM), Workday (WDAY), and AppFolio (APPF). Veritone trades even more cheaply than HubSpot (HUBS), which has been in the penalty box all year:

That discount, in my view, reflects choppy execution on Veritone's part. Key for investors to watch will be if the company can sustain its current sequential improvements in software revenue growth and consumption ARR, as well as evidence that it can convert its $40+ million VDR pipeline into new bookings. So far, Q3 results look like incredible progress; hence, my rating upgrade on this stock: though Veritone has exhibited very lumpy, unreliable results in the past.

I still prefer to remain on the sidelines here, though improving performance certainly piques my interest to watch VERI stock more closely for a potential buy opportunity.

data.title: Trevi Therapeutics: "Buy" Rating For Haduvio Treating Chronic Cough In IPF And Beyond
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Trevi Therapeutics (TRVI) with a "Buy" rating, and the reason why is because it is already ahead of the game in terms of getting its lead program into phase 3 testing. I'm talking about the development of Haduvio [oral nalbuphine ER] for the treatment of cough in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Consider that the company has already been able to achieve positive topline results from the phase 2b CORAL study and is expected to file a meeting request with the FDA in Q4 of 2025. This is a crucial inflection point because if talks go well, then the company should be able to initiate a phase 3 study using Haduvio for the treatment of IPF patients with chronic cough in the 1st half of 2026. Bottom line is that this program is important because it is an unmet need, as there is currently no FDA-approved therapy for a majority of these IPF patients with chronic cough.

What puts this company in an even better position is that its drug Haduvio has not only achieved positive results in the phase 2b CORAL study treating chronic cough in patients with IPF. It has also been able to evaluate the use of it successfully in the phase 2a RIVER study treating patients with refractory chronic cough [RCC]. The point is that this is the only company with an investigational drug that has been able to achieve positive phase 2 data in treating chronic cough both in IPF and RCC patients. In terms of the positive data from the RIVER study, this will now allow the company to initiate a phase 2b study targeting RCC patients in the 1st half of 2026.

One reason to go over this company would be the positive data it achieved from both of the phase 2 studies, treating chronic cough in both IPF and RCC patients, respectively. The second reason is because it just released its Q3 of 2025 financial results and earnings update, noting that it has an expected cash runway into 2028. The final reason, which I believe sets this biotech up well with the positive phase 2 data it had reported to date, is that there was speculation of a potential buyer having takeover interest of this company. It makes sense, because chronic coughing in IPF patients is an unmet medical need with no FDA-approved therapies. Plus, it has a differentiated mode of action compared to other drugs in having both central and peripheral targeting.

Positive Phase 2b Data Leads Way To Possible Phase 3 Study In 2026

As I noted above, the company was able to achieve positive results from its phase 2b CORAL study using its drug Haduvio [oral nalbuphine ER] for the treatment of chronic cough in patients with Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis [IPF]. Before going over the positive data that it obtained from that mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe it is first important to understand what this disorder actually is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Pulmonary Fibrosis is characterized as a progressive, chronic disease of the lungs that is a result of fibrosis. This type of fibrosis causes inflammation of the lungs, and it also makes it hard for a patient to breathe. The term "idiopathic" means it results due to an unknown cause. There are two scenarios here with respect to IPF, which is that there are no symptoms present at all at first. However, as this disease progresses, then there are a host of symptoms that these patients experience. What's even worse is that IPF actually can lead to two other issues, which are:

  • Pulmonary Hypertension - blood pressure of the lungs increases to a higher than expected level
  • Respiratory failure - the lungs are unable to pump out enough oxygen to the brain and other organs leading to this type of failure
