Currently available therapies aren't really that great. Why is that? They help, of course, but they only slow down the scarring [fibrosis] of the lungs. They are not actually cures for IPF. One drug approved to slow down disease progression is Esbriet [pirfenidone] from Legacy Pharma with U.S. rights and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) with rights outside of the United States. The other two drugs are Ofev [nintedanib] and Jascayd [nerandomilast] from Boehringer Ingelheim.

Okay, but where does a drug from Trevi Therapeutics fit in? The company's goal is to alleviate a major issue associated with IPF and Interstitial Lung Disease [ILD], which is coughing. Consider that with approximately 150,000 IPF patients in the United States, approximately 85% of them are plagued with chronic coughing [Trevi Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #6]. Not only does it interfere in their daily activities, but the coughing itself exacerbates the problem of disease progression. Chronic cough leads to further damage of the lungs. This is where Haduvio from the company comes into play, where it targets the entire cough reflex arc pathway. Specifically, it targets this pathway both centrally and peripherally. How so? By targeting opioid receptors through different aspects, which are Kappa agonists and Mu antagonists. This occurs with the following:

  • Kappa agonism - activating receptors of the central nervous system [CNS] and peripheral nerves - in turn reducing cough frequency
  • Mu receptor agonist - to reduce cough

Both kappa agonism and mu antagonism have the effect of reducing cough, but both are put together to reduce cough without activating the mu-opioid receptors [mu-opioid receptors are known to cause sedation and abuse potential]. The "central" brain of it reduced chronic cough hypersensitization [need to react to coughing] and then the "peripheral" limits signals from the lungs to the brain. As you can see, this company has a unique mechanism of action [MOA] of acting both centrally and peripherally. Only "peripheral" targeting has failed in chronic cough for IPF patients [Trevi Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #7]. Those drugs that are only "fibrotic" targeting have not shown a benefit towards improving a patient's cough. With no FDA-approved therapies and current options being limited in potential, there is room for Trevi to target a majority of IPF patients that have chronic cough. The company is focused on reducing chronic cough for IPF, but the beauty is that it could possibly eventually expand the population it could go after. Besides IPF, there are non-IPF patients with Interstitial Lung Disease [ILD] who might benefit from treatment with Haduvio. Uncontrolled cough is observed in 50% to 60% of non-IPF ILD patients. Thus, the point is that this is an expansion opportunity that it could shift to, should it achieve great success in a potential phase 3 study.

In order to determine whether or not Haduvio would be effective in reducing cough frequency in patients with IPF, the company ran the phase 2b CORAL study. This trial was split up into three parts as follows:

  • 2-week titration period
  • 4-week double-blind fixed-dose period
  • 2-week follow-up period

The primary endpoint was to evaluate the relative change from baseline in 24-hour cough frequency versus placebo at Week-6. The truth is that the drug ended up being ideal for all of the doses tested. Specifically, during the double-blind fixed-dosed period [4-weeks] patients were randomized to receive one of the following doses:

  • 27 mg BID [twice-daily] dosing of Haduvio
  • 54 mg BID dosing of Haduvio
  • 108 mg BID dosing of Haduvio
  • Placebo BID dosing

All doses tested achieved a statistically significant [CHEST 2025 Annual Meeting Results Phase 2b CORAL Study, slide #6] relative change from baseline of drug versus placebo in terms of 24-hour cough frequency at Week-6. The highest dose achieved the best placebo-adjusted reduction of -43.3% [-60.2% for 108 mg of Haduvio and then -16.9% for placebo].

Another positive to consider is that Trevi did a drug-drug interaction study in healthy adult participants. Why would it run a study of this caliber? The whole point was to see that if it combined Haduvio with standard of care [SOC] antifibrotic drugs, there would be no systemic issues or negative reaction. With no negative pharmacokinetic [PK] findings observed, this was another positive development for this company. The whole point of bringing up this biotech and this specific program of developing this drug as a chronic cough treatment for patients with IPF is that there are two major catalysts to keep an eye on within a 1-year period. One catalyst of which is that the company is gearing up to request an end-of-phase 2 [EOP2] meeting with the FDA in Q4 of 2025 to discuss the next stage of clinical development for this program. The second catalyst, of which, if there is a general consensus of the FDA meeting, would be the actual initiation of the phase 3 study using Haduvio for chronic cough patients with IPF in the 1st half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC filing, Trevi Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $194.9 million as of September 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand was because of an offering that the company did back on June 5, 2025. That is when it issued and sold 17,400,000 shares of its common stock at an offering price of $5.75 per share. In addition, it gave the underwriters a 30-day option to exercise up to an additional 2,610,000 shares of common stock at this same public offering price. The underwriters did indeed exercise their option in full; with that being said, the total aggregate gross proceeds from this offering were $115.1 million. The truth is that this company is in excellent shape in terms of cash. That's because the cash runway projection it laid out is that the funds it has will be enough to operate its business into 2028. The company burns roughly $13.9 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $10.1 million in R&D expenses and then $3.8 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Trevi Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the request for the FDA EOP2 meeting in Q4 of 2025. The risk here is that even though the company attained statistically significant results from the phase 2b CORAL study using Haduvio for chronic cough of patients with IPF, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to greenlight or allow the initiation of a phase 3 study. Even if a late-stage study is allowed for this indication, upon completion of it, there is no assurance that the primary endpoint is going to end up being met in a statistically significant manner.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the company evaluating the use of Haduvio for another chronic cough condition. This would be its program of using this drug to treat patients with refractory chronic cough [RCC]. The intention of Trevi is that it wants to initiate a phase 2b study treating these chronic cough patients. The risk here is that even though the company achieved positive results from its phase 2a RIVER trial for this program, there is no guarantee that it will achieve a similar or superior outcome in the potential initiation of the phase 2b study targeting these patients with RCC using Haduvio.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of a possible expansion opportunity. By this, I'm talking about the company potentially shifting over to evaluate the use of Haduvio for the approximately 50%-60% of 228,000 U.S. patients with uncontrolled chronic cough who have non-IPF ILD. The company states in its 10-Q SEC filing [page 21] that it intends to start a clinical trial for these non-IPF ILD patients who both have chronic cough and underlying fibrosis. The risk here is that even if it initiates a trial of this caliber, there is no assurance that Haduvio is going to achieve a positive outcome from this trial. That is, it is not guaranteed that it will work to reduce cough frequency or somehow improve fibrosis for these patients.

Conclusion

Trevi Therapeutics is already set up for long-term success. Of course, it still has a lot that it needs to do. For instance, it truly needs to complete a phase 3 program so that it could eventually obtain FDA approval for its drug Haduvio. Speaking of which, this is where the two catalysts I noted above come in. The first of which is the EOP2 meeting with the FDA, expected to be requested in Q4 of 2025. Should that meeting go well, then the company is going to be able to initiate a phase 3 study using Haduvio for the treatment of chronic cough in IPF patients. The good thing about this company is its alternate approach to treating chronic cough. That is its unique MOA of targeting the cough reflex arc, both centrally and peripherally.

In addition, it is already in a good position in terms of expanding towards other indications with this very same drug. This would be in terms of reducing chronic cough in both non-IPF ILD and RCC patients. What also really sets this company up well is that there are no FDA-approved therapies for reducing chronic cough in IPF patients. This adds to the speculation that there might be a takeover interest of this company by another due to the prospects that it has in place. With the potential to initiate a phase 3 study for Haduvio for the treatment of chronic cough in IPF patients in 2026, plus the ability to move towards a phase 2b study of the very same drug targeting non-IPF ILD patients, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a "Buy" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nike Before Q2: The Turnaround Story Everyone Wants, But Still Can't Quite See
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I wrote about Nike, Inc. (NKE) was at the end of June, when they reported their fiscal Q4 and full-year results. Back then, the whole narrative was very much about whether we were at the very beginning of a turnaround or just looking at a temporary pause in a pretty ugly downtrend. I was cautiously optimistic in that piece, but I was also very clear that while I saw some early indications that the turnaround might start happening, I did not see enough to say “this is it, the turnaround is here.” It felt much more like Nike had had a really, really bad couple of years where a lot of internal mistakes collided with external headwinds at exactly the wrong time, and maybe, just maybe, they were finally done falling and starting to operate better and fix some of what they broke.

Because of all the risks I discussed back then - execution, tariffs, still high valuation - I said I would stay on the sidelines, and that while the possibility of a turnaround was there, I couldn’t honestly say the turnaround was already happening.

Now we’re a few weeks away from fiscal Q2 results on December 18th. I completely skipped fiscal Q1 in terms of proper coverage, and this makes it an interesting moment to come back to the name and see where things stand. This is essentially an earnings preview for Q2, with the benefit of hindsight on Q1 and a fresh comparison to what I wrote in June.

Before writing this piece, I went back and reread my previous article, and I have to say it was very interesting to compare what I said then to what the company did over the past few months. From a performance standpoint, if we look at the stock since that late June article, Nike has delivered a total return of about minus 7.5%, including the dividend paid, but not reinvested, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up almost 11% over the same period. So, purely from a portfolio perspective, I am pretty happy with my Hold rating, and I would definitely say that staying on the sidelines back then was the right call.

Recent Headlines

The more interesting question is what is happening right now because Nike actually managed to surprise me with some of the recent headlines. First, they raised the dividend not so long ago, which is always a deliberate signal from management about confidence and long-term cash generation.

Second, they announced a partnership with Skims, and as a female Gen Z representative, I know exactly how huge that is and how big Skims is in terms of demand and brand power. Skims already has massive sales, and I remember how, a year and a half ago in New York, I was desperately trying to find a physical store to buy something from them, only to discover that it wasn’t open yet. I never managed to buy their products, even though I really wanted to.

The campaigns Skims runs on social media are incredibly powerful, and even if you completely ignore the Kardashian angle (and in my case, you can because I’m not from the U.S., I’m not a fan, I never watched their show, and I have no idea who they are and what they do), I still know a lot about Skims as a brand. I have seen their products and promotions so many times on social media that it’s obvious why this business is doing so well. Some of the products look genuinely thoughtful for women, and you can clearly see there is a reason behind the growth. So when Nike announced a partnership with Skims, that definitely caught my attention in a good way.

At the same time, alongside these “nice” headlines, Nike is also reshuffling its senior leadership in a pretty dramatic way. We have a new COO (this position did not exist before), they eliminated the CTO and CCO roles, and as much as this all fits into the turnaround narrative and sends a signal to the market that Nike is “trying,” my opinion on these moves is mixed.

On one hand, this absolutely supports the idea that management is not asleep at the wheel - they are clearly aware of the problems, they are taking visible steps, and they do want investors to see that they are changing things that didn’t work. On every earnings call recently, they’ve repeated that they fully understand the issues and are actively working to fix them, and the dividend increase plus leadership shake-up is consistent with that message.

On the other hand, if I look at the concrete details of what they’ve done with the org chart, it doesn’t look straightforward to me at all. I can’t tell you that it all makes perfect sense.

The company has created a new COO, and into that role they moved their Chief Supply Chain Officer, Venkatesh Alagirisamy. In his new position, he is supposed to lead technology as well as everything he already handled before, including supply chain, planning, operations, manufacturing, and sustainability, and by doing that, he is supposed to ensure that technology is fully integrated across the company. But if we really think about this, it’s not that he suddenly jumped several levels in responsibility. He was already a C-level executive, and now he’s a C-level executive with a slightly different title and more things to do. He basically went from CSCO to COO.

The more confusing part is that, under this reshuffling, they eliminated the role of Chief Technology Officer altogether, and the CTO left. As someone who is very interested in everything related to digital, technology, and the specific problems Nike has had in this area, I find this decision very hard to interpret without more explanation. I focused heavily on their digital strategy in my previous article precisely because it was such a weak spot, and I honestly do not see, at least for now, how putting technology under a former supply chain head instead of having a dedicated CTO will help.

So for now, without more detailed commentary from management, I cannot say this leadership shuffle will do a lot of good. On paper, it really sounds like the management wants to show that they are working in the right direction and that they are “integrating” things. The phrase that he will be ensuring that technology is fully integrated across the company immediately makes me wonder what the previous CTO was doing and whether this heavy digital underperformance is partly the result of technology not having the right leadership earlier.

In other words, at this point, I have many more questions than answers. It could absolutely turn out to be a positive change over time, but investors should understand that right now it looks more like a move to reassure investors that things are moving in the right direction.

Financials

I think it’s useful to go back to what I said I would be watching after Q4 and to check where things landed after Q1 because that gives us the context we need going into Q2. In the June article, I explained in detail how Nike got to where it is now. If you are not familiar with the story, you can go back to that piece and read the section “What Went Wrong Before This Quarter,” because I break it down there very thoroughly.

The short version is that Nike made a series of pretty bad internal decisions at the same time as the U.S. market was going through tougher import policies, rising cost pressures, and China-related setbacks. Their push into digital didn’t work at the scale they needed, but digital was becoming a growth engine for many other companies, so Nike was losing that battle.

Back then, I said that some of the changes we saw in Q4 looked like early progress and could point to a potential turnaround in the future. The specific metrics I wanted to watch were margins, digital performance, wholesale recovery, inventory trends, and SG&A expenses.

On margins, I said I wanted to see stabilization or eventual improvement. We are not there yet. If anything, what we’ve seen so far is stabilization of the decline, not stabilization at a healthy level. In Q1, gross margin decreased 320 basis points to 42.2%, and if you look at the guidance for Q2, the company expects margins to decline a further 300 to 375 basis points, including a 175 basis-point headwind from new tariffs. So you can argue it’s not getting incrementally worse quarter after quarter, so that’s some form of stabilization, but obviously, what I would like to see is margins stabilizing at a respectable level.

We expect Q2 gross margins to be down approximately 300 to 375 basis points including a net headwind of 175 basis points from the new incremental tariffs.

On digital, I said it was a very weak spot back in the summer, but in Q1, it did not just stay weak, it pretty much collapsed. Nike Digital was down 12% in the first quarter, which is worse than I personally expected. Wholesale, on the other hand, grew 7% and clearly was the star of the show, which is exactly the directional improvement I highlighted as likely. The market expected revenues to decline mid-single digits, but instead revenue grew 1%. That was a positive surprise and helped the stock, but it doesn’t change the fact that the quality of that growth is very mixed.

Inventory is an area where things are slowly moving in the right direction. In Q4, inventory started to improve, and in Q1, inventories were down 2% year-over-year. That is a major green flag, and if they can continue to trim inventory, it will be one of the most important building blocks for a healthier Nike. So, looking at my expectations back in time, I said Q1 could be disappointing, especially on revenue, and I thought the topline would be weaker, but instead, revenue surprised to the upside. Although margins shrank. Taken together, it still doesn’t look like a company in the middle of a turnaround.

One of the more interesting details is SG&A. In Q4, I was worried that they might overspend as they tried to repair the brand, and I said we could see SG&A rising as they poured money into marketing and “fixing things.” Instead, in Q1, SG&A declined. That naturally raises the question of whether they are deliberately underspending to make the numbers look better in the short term and support the turnaround narrative, or whether they are genuinely becoming more disciplined and efficient. Obviously, the second option would be much better, but the first one is not out of the question either.

Guidance

If you listen to the Q1 call, the message from management is pretty similar to what we heard in Q4. They say that they are aware of the issues:

Nike's journey back to greatness has only just begun. There is significant work ahead, especially in the areas of Sportswear, Greater China and Nike Direct. And as I've said to the team, progress won't be perfectly linear, but the direction is.

Management says they believe they can return to double-digit margins, and when they talk about double-digit margins, they refer to operating margins. Historically, Nike did sit comfortably in the range of around 11–14% on average, but over the last two years, operating margins have dropped to about 7%. The company insists it is possible to get back to double digits and points to their own history to justify that view, basically saying that with the right mix of business, better product and channel mix, the right geography and segment balance, they should be able to lift margins again. Their plan is to do that by reigniting organic growth, improving the full-price mix, and eventually benefiting from operating leverage as the business grows.

Directionally, this all sounds great. Every investor would love to see operating margins back in the low teens with solid organic growth and a cleaner digital business. But at the same time, no one should feel bad for asking whether this is realistic given how things have looked in the last few quarters, why the company wasn’t able to maintain those margins before, and what exactly will change now to make this time different. It’s one thing to say “we believe we can do it,” and completely another thing to show the execution.

For Q2, Nike expects revenues to be down low single digits, essentially the same kind of guidance they gave for Q1, which they then beat. Margins, as mentioned, are expected to decline by 300–375 basis points, including the 175-basis-point headwind from incremental tariffs. The tax rate for Q2 and the full year is expected to be in the low 20% range.

They say momentum with wholesale is strong, and as a result, they expect wholesale revenue to return to modest growth for fiscal 2026. That part looks believable and very helpful for the turnaround story.

At the same time, they do not expect Nike Direct to return to growth in fiscal 2026, even though they talk a lot about initiatives to boost that part of the business. SG&A is expected to grow low single digits for the year, which again tells you that they are not planning some wild spending spree to fix everything overnight, but also that the path to higher margins will not be driven by a massive cut in investments either. It’s a slow, grinding process.

Valuation & Conclusion

The last piece of the puzzle is valuation, and this is where my issues with this company become even heavier. Nike is still very expensive, and the fact that the stock jumped 20% on a “better than expected” earnings report that was not good fundamentally says a lot about how hungry the market is for a feel-good turnaround story here.

The same thing is happening now on a smaller scale - the stock rises on management changes and partnership announcements that look more cosmetic (at least for now) than transformational at this stage. The market clearly wants to believe in a clean turnaround and is ready to buy if the company starts delivering numbers that support that narrative. If Nike genuinely starts showing real turnaround in the numbers - not just small revenue beats with shrinking margins and collapsing digital, but real margin stabilization, digital recovering, China improving - I think investors will absolutely buy the stock even at a rich valuation.

For me, this creates a very weird situation. Unless the stock drops meaningfully from current levels, I don’t see myself buying it, even though I’m not arguing that it should trade right in line with the sector. You can absolutely make the case that Nike deserves a premium because the dividend is there and has been increased, the brand is incredibly strong, they are not heavily leveraged, they have plenty of cash, and, well, it’s Nike.

But still, with the way the numbers look today, I would need either a much more convincing turnaround in the fundamentals (or even a straightforward growth with a nice slope showing it won’t go away), or a much lower entry point to get interested. Realistically, I would be looking for another 20–30% downside from here, combined with evidence that the turnaround is actually happening, not just talked about on calls. Without that, I don’t see a compelling setup for me personally.

In my opinion, the stock can go down even further based on valuation alone, especially if the actual results fail to support the bullish narrative and the optimism coming from management, but I don’t expect it to go as low as I would like it to go to be attractive. So just like in June, I will be staying aside and following the Nike story, it’s for sure an interesting one.

data.title: Strive: Bitcoin Treasury - Opportunity Or Trap?
data.description:
I rate Strive Asset Management, LLC (NASDAQ:ASST) a Strong Sell, for serious investors looking to avoid speculative stocks with no earnings or profits. The company promotes itself as a Bitcoin treasury manager with the added positioning of highly political rhetoric including pushback against such themes as ESG and Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion. Its promotion of capitalistic philosophy is at odds with the facts, given the ultra-low stock price, lack of profits and level of short interest.

Q3 financial results were horrific in my view. Not only did the company declare an expected significant loss, but it also announced it would buy Semler Scientific to get into the preventative healthcare space, raising serious questions about its business model.

The question becomes is this company going to continue to burn cash or does it provide an opportunity for the current investor to snap up shares for a discount, hoping for a turnaround? The Trump administration has been more friendly to the Bitcoin industry due to donations, so some optimism behind its chances has some basis. The conclusion is this is a highly speculative stock, which mixes Bitcoin, politics, and young executives with bold visions of capitalism and fueling portfolios with crypto...and medical devices.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Stock is down -85% in the last 6 months, trading at $1.04 per share.
  • Does not currently pay a dividend.
  • Loses money, has no earnings.
  • Has a short interest of 15.19% indicating investors are betting against it, in my opinion.
  • Originally founded by Vivek Ramaswamy, the former Presidential candidate, who resigned a year later.
  • Bitcoin hit an all-time high of $126,000 on October 6 but has since dropped back down to $91,975.
  • Q3 declared a GAAP net loss of -$192.3 million.
  • Q3 declared an acquisition of a scientific company to get into the preventative healthcare space.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the stock.

The chart below captures the story fairly well in my view. A decline of -94% since inception, $856 million in market capitalization, with a stock price of $1.04. In my experience, stocks under $5 tend to be in crisis, such as when Lehman Brothers went bankrupt during the Global Financial Crisis, and yet, some investors continued to buy the stock based on the optimistic view it still held value.

Bitcoin Treasury Management

Strive was founded in 2022 by Vivek Ramaswamy (former Presidential candidate) and Anson Frericks. It is headquartered in Dallas, Texas. In 2023 Ramaswamy resigned from the company to pursue his campaign for president, and two years later it went public.

ASST positions itself as the first publicly traded asset management firm to use a Bitcoin treasury strategy amplified with perpetual preferred equity. Its Vision is to integrate Bitcoin into retail and institutional portfolios, expanding from ETFs to Bitcoin Bonds. The company also plans to capitalize on retirement portfolio’s where recently a Trump executive order allows investors to invest in alternative investments such as Bitcoin in their retirement accounts.

Within their portfolio, the company offers preferred shares under symbol (SATA), with a 12% coupon rate, yielding 14.8% at today’s pricing.

Q3 Financial Results

On November 14, the company declared Q3 results, with the following financial highlights:

  • GAAP Net loss of -$192.3 million, or -$0.22 per share.
  • Projected loss of single digit million-dollar loss to a single digit million-dollar income in 2026.
  • Owns 5,886 Bitcoin, with a total cost of $683 million and fair value of $672.9 million as of 9/30/25.
  • Announced an acquisition of Semler Scientific in an all-stock transaction, which would make it a wholly owned subsidiary.

According to the press release, the acquisition of a medical device company will be a good fit with a Bitcoin company:

We also entered into an agreement and plan of merger to acquire Semler Scientific, Inc., which we expect will be accretive to the Bitcoin exposure to our shareholders and provide significant potential future value based on the synergy of Semler's medical device business and the expertise of Strive management and board members.

Overall, I have never seen such a collection of red flags to be wary of. In my experience, the management of this company is over confident and doesn’t understand portfolio strategy. The mixture of Bitcoin and medical devices doesn’t make any sense, even if one were to believe the board members were talented individuals.

Under Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 in ASST since inception in 2022, would be worth $586 today, that’s a loss of -$9,414.

Valuation

ASST has an enterprise value of $750.8 million, with a market capitalization of $856.3 million. Total debt is $3.6 million, while cash is $109.07 million. The company does not have a price/earnings ratio because it has no earnings.

Outlook

ASST management declared 2026 would either lose just over -$1 million or create an income in the “single digit million dollar” area. In my view, this statement highlights the speculative nature of the investment. It's like flipping a coin, it could be good or really bad. While it is true that Bitcoin can be volatile, it doesn't inspire confidence in senior management, in my opinion.

Risks To Rating

ASST is a high-risk speculative stock. To date, the company has only declared losses, with the stock price reflecting a boom and bust of optimism and fear behind the company’s potential. The extreme volatility favors the bold trader, who is willing to take significant risks, but it does not demonstrate a company valued on its fundamentals.

Other risks include its business model, which is currently unproven. ASST places a large emphasis on holding and acquiring Bitcoin assets, which as an asset class itself is still rather new and volatile. In my view, Bitcoin pricing is driven by investor sentiment, going up in value during positive sentiment, then dropping at elevator speed upon bad news.

Reputation risk is another factor. As large institutions choose suppliers for pension and retirement portfolios, the question becomes how ASST will distinguish themselves among such large industry sponsors such as BlackRock, Fidelity, and Brookfield Asset Management.

Bottom Line

I rate Strive Asset Management, LLC – ASST as a Strong Sell, cautioning all investors that it is a highly speculative stock. The recently released Q3 financial highlights showed a significant loss and plans for this asset manager to buy a medical device company, which highlights their lack of a solid strategy.

Within the company’s history and communication, it emphasizes political views with a focus on capitalism yet isn’t a role model. In fact, the only people who seem to be making money are the executives who were paid “share-based compensation expense” despite the loss. Originally founded by Vivek Ramaswamy and Anson Frericks in 2022, Vivek resigned a year later in 2023 to run for President.

ASST is down in price -94% since inception. The stock trades at just over a dollar ($1.04). It doesn’t pay a dividend or have any earnings. The short interest is currently 15.19% indicating a group of investors are betting heavily against it.

Bottom line, ASST is a coin flip, in my view. It could be driven higher by Bitcoin enthusiasm or be forced to sell off its assets to get out of the business.
